data.title: Nvidia: All-Time High Is In Sight, Upgrade To Strong Buy
data.description:
Introduction

When I covered Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) at the tail-end of last year, I thought the stock was well set up going into 2025. Valuations had come down despite a furious run in the shares as earnings growth blistered ahead, buoyed by seemingly endless hyperscaler demand for GPUs. With 2025 set to be the year of Blackwell, I expected the stock to continue performing, albeit at more moderate pace than in the immediately preceding years.

Instead, we saw the stock briefly attempt to break to a new all-time high, or ATH, before a sharp turnaround and sell off. We know the various reasons for the reversal. On one hand the broader market sold off amidst tariff uncertainty leaving very few unscathed. The release of DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) in the early part of 2025 led to a sort of flash panic, with claims that the boom in high end chips was over. The logic was DeepSeek would allow for such cost saving and efficiency that companies running AI inference could rely on lesser chips. I think the evidence at this point is that line of reasoning was just plain wrong, instead we saw the Jevons Paradox ring true.

Nvidia has also essentially now lost all China related revenue, something which perhaps was inevitable. Speaking at a recent conference, CFO Colette Kress pretty much threw cold water on the prospects of any further China revenue for the firm.

“Now that means where we stand is in a situation that we really don't have anything for that market. We've discussed that it wouldn't be appropriate for us to just start a new chip at this point because essentially, the H20 from where it compared to our Blackwell architecture was significantly lower in terms of what we were being able to enable in China. That was about a 25x change from an H20 to what you would receive in terms of a Blackwell.” (Colette Kress, CFO.)

With those headwinds consider, Nvidia has essentially gone nowhere since my last coverage as you can see from the below chart.

The price performance has indeed been a disappointment, but while the stock has been weighed by narratives such as DeepSeek, the earnings have just kept on grinding higher. The chart below shows the next twelve month, NTM EPS for Nvidia based on the consensus analyst forecast. I have annotated to show where EPS was at the time of my last coverage. As you can see its almost like a stairs, it has been consistently and periodically moving higher at near fixed intervals, despite the struggles in the stock's price. As a result of the lack of action in the price, the earnings growth has meant the valuation of the stock looks outright cheap.

The mixture of an extremely attractive valuation and a shifting narrative I believe sets Nvidia up nicely to have a better H2. The price momentum appears to be coming back into the stock as continued earnings growth, hyperscaler capex commentary and the new dynamic of sovereign AI has helped bring buying back into the name. I think Nvidia is well positioned heading into H2 and far beyond, after a period of consolidation, and I think the next leg is upon us. As a result, I am feeling even more confident and moving Nvidia to a Strong Buy rating.

Shareholder returns

As I see it Nvidia's valuation is overwhelmingly attractive. Its two best performing peers in the Magnificent-7 this year have been Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) and Meta (NASDAQ:META). Looking at how Nvidia stacks up with these peers I see a great dispersion at present. Nvidia trades smack bang in the middle of the three in terms of its P/E ratio, but on the basis of both Return on Invested Capital and medium term EPS forecasts it is way ahead.

On the ROIC point, I think its important to remind readers why the metric is so important and what Nvidia's 80% return tells us about it. So I refer to the wonderful work of Michael Mauboussin from Morgan Stanley, who has written elegantly on this topic.

“A company creates value when the present value of the cash flows from its investments are greater than the cost of the investments. In other words, one dollar invested in the business becomes worth more than one dollar in the market.”

A company generates value for its shareholders when the return on the capital invested in the business exceeds the cost of capital, which is usually expressed as the weighted average cost of capital or WACC. That of course being the respective weights of debt and equity capital used in the business and their respective costs. On average for a high quality large cap name the WACC is likely somewhere between 8-12%.

So for simplicity sake let just assume for Nvidia the WACC is 10%, and the company as we know is generating a ROIC of 80%. This tells us that for every one dollar invested Nvidia is creating eight dollars of shareholder value. This kind of wealth creation is simply bananas. This is the hallmark of a "once in a generation" business, which is underpinned by one of the great investment cycles of our time, the AI revolution.

We know Nvidia has been powered ahead since 2022 by the huge capex cycle at the hyperscale cloud players. Each of which has thrown money hand over fist to secure GPUs to train proprietary AI models and serve inference on those models. This cycle shows no end in sight as the big tech players during the most recent earnings season reaffirmed their plans. For instance, Alphabet/Google (GOOG, GOOGL) is forking out $75bn this year, which would represent a huge 42% increase from 2024, which in turn saw a >60% capex increase from 2023. The pace of spending is ferocious.

On top of this demand, we now add in the new demand coming from sovereign AI. Last month we saw CEO Jensen Huang in the Middle East inking deals with Saudi Arabia and the UAE. Under these deals, Nvidia will provide 500,000 GPUs per year until 2027 to the UAE in partnership with domestic firms like G42. That's a lot of GPUs. The Saudi deal stipulated an initial deployment of 18,000 cutting edge Blackwell GB300s as part of a partnership with the new national AI company Humain. An announcement which one would imagine is simply the first step in the process given the scale of the UAE deal.

On the sovereign side I think this is the tip of the iceberg. The genie of U.S. tech proliferation is now out of the bottle following the loosening of restrictions on tech export to trusted partners. Nvidia does have to contend with the likelihood of never selling another chip to China but given this new wave of demand hitting the market, I think they are more than compensated.

Don't fall prey to bias

As the wonderful behavioral economist Richard Thaler set out, humans are prone to an array of cognitive bias. These are in essence, consistent mistakes we make in our judgment of the world, they are a function of our need to think quickly about the world, availing of so-called heuristics or short cuts to answers when evaluating the world.

This is relevant to Nvidia because of a thing called anchoring bias. This is a common cognitive error whereby we have a tendency to anchor predictions to currently available limits or levels, such as a price. Just look at any economists forecast and you'll see what I mean, generally the forecast is always some small variation on the current paradigm. It matters in the case of Nvidia because we are on the cusp of an all-time high and a valuation of greater than $3.5 trillion.

The human tendency, and bias, is to say, wow, 3.5 trillion, that's a big number. How much further can it go from here. The market cap of Nvidia is already close to the GDP of the UK. However, history teaches us these points of resistance are imaginary. Back in 2010, the largest stock in the S&P 500 (SP500) was Exxon Mobil (NYSE:XOM), which at the time boasted a market cap of around $315bn, and it was more than $50bn larger than second place Microsoft (MSFT), a huge margin at the time.

Investors at the time probably wondered realistically how much larger the biggest stocks could get. Exxon in this context has performed relatively poorly, rising just 53% since 2010. Microsoft, on the other hand, has seen a greater than 10x growth since then. If you had said to the average investor in 2010 that Microsoft would be over $3.5 trillion just 15 years later, you might have been laughed at or at least told to “be realistic.” The reason being, we are so grounded in the data in front of us today it can be hard to break the mold.

Despite a $3.5 trillion market cap today, I see absolutely no reason why Nvidia couldn't be worth more than $10 trillion either in 5 or 10 years time. The stock is generating unreal returns on shareholder capital, its P/E ratio is very low by the company's own history and somehow priced in line with big tech peers despite Nvidia forecast to deliver much higher EPS growth. As I see it the valuation looks really supportive of a strong buy recommendation here.

Risks

The biggest risk for Nvidia is the durability of capex spending. Decisions around capex traditionally at least have followed the economic cycle, companies opt to spend more when the going is good and pull back on spending in shakier times. The capex boom at hyperscalers has been a spending cycle like no other and thus far has shown little indication of letting up.

Of course, at some point the capex cycle will have to moderate. The question is to what degree. I think its unlikely we would see a sharp decline in capex spending, especially given the game theory dynamic of needing to spend if your rivals are spending, who in turn feel they need to spend if they think you will.

Competition remains a potential threat even if Nvidia appears to have a huge lead which is expected to persist. History tells the story many times over of market leaders assured of their success who grow complacent, see IBM (NASDAQ:IBM) in the 1980s or Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) more recently as examples.

Conclusion

I am pushing my recommendation on Nvidia to a Strong Buy. The stock has done very little from a price perspective since I covered it last. However, during this sideways action the underlying EPS growth has continued to grind higher, resulting in a very attractive P/E entry point. The sheer profitability of the company is a marvel and despite being one of the most valuable companies in the world today I think the runway remains in place for years to come.

The company has surfed the AI wave, underpinned by massive capex from hyperscalers pitted against each other for AI dominance. Now the market is expanding into the sovereign space with deals in Saudi Arabia and the UAE, deals which I believe will elongate one of the great capital investment cycles of our time. Nvidia remains the clear AI leader, competition is on the rise but remains a few steps behind. With the Rubin and Feynman chips already planned, I expect Nvidia will at least keep pace with competitors if not further extend their lead given a proven execution of product road maps.

data.title: Cutting-Edge Momentum: Steven Cress's Top AI Stocks
data.description:
Steven Cress here—and welcome to Seeking Alpha Quant. In this article, I’ll discuss my top five stocks powering new innovations in artificial intelligence. Each one has demonstrated remarkable resilience in the face of volatile markets and has solid fundamentals to back up its Strong Buy rating. Let’s dive in.

AI Generates Big Money and Big Deals in Silicon Valley

As we meet the halfway point of 2025, artificial intelligence innovations continue defying the status quo of volatile markets.

AI is fuelling big money and big deals in Silicon Valley and beyond. Microsoft (MSFT)-backed OpenAI nearly doubled its annual recurring revenue run rate to $10B as of June,, following explosive demand for ChatGPT and reports that it will tap OpenAI Alphabet's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google cloud service in an unprecedented deal to turbocharge its computing abilities.

OpenAI’s growing financial prowess echoes that of other AI trailblazers such as coding tool Cursor, which hit $500M in ARR this year from less than $100M in 2024, and Anthropic, which tripled its ARR to $3B this year, according to The Financial Times. Investors can’t get enough: OpenAI is currently raising $40B from SoftBank and other deep-pocketed backers, while Anthropic has gleaned investment from GOOG, Amazon (AMZN), and top venture capital firms like Lightspeed Venture Partners, Menlo Ventures, and General Catalyst.

Meanwhile, Meta (META) plans to invest $15B in San Francisco-based Scale AI in one of the biggest M&A deals of its kind as it seeks to catch up with GOOG, MSFT, AMZN, and its other AI-leveraging Big Tech adversaries.

Okay… But How Can The Average Investor Get Involved?

You don’t have to be a chief financial officer of a tech juggernaut, portfolio manager at a private equity giant, or venture capitalist to profit from AI’s lucrative rise. The latest and greatest innovations in cloud computing, machine learning, big data analytics, and other AI functions are percolating through the public market, transforming the software and tech companies that harness its power into moneymaking machines.

Enterprise technology spending has been growing at an average annual rate of 8% in the U.S. since 2022, according to a McKinsey study, reflecting AI technology's increasing role in businesses’ ability to scale, compete, and build value. The numbers make clear that advanced technology is what currently separates the strong from the weak in corporate America: companies with high-performing IT systems have exhibited 35% higher revenue growth and 10% higher profit margins than their competitors, per a separate McKinsey analysis.

McKinsey surveyed business leaders on their use of 50 different tech capabilities, then correlated those responses to company profit margins. “As expected, digital-native tech companies and start-ups—not often included in such research in the past—surpassed their traditional industry peers in IT maturity,” McKinsey concluded. “But some companies in traditional industries, including global energy and materials, banking, and telecom, are closing the gap.”

The research backs something I often preach when it comes to investing: whoever wields the AI wields the power. Whether it be cutting-edge tech operations or companies hailing from more traditional industries that have, as McKinsey points out, begun to leverage AI throughout their organizational structures, the companies growing the most from AI don’t always fit the mold of what you might consider an AI-first operation.

My list of top AI stocks reflects this trend, offering a blend of major tech companies, lesser-known mid-cap enterprises making waves in the world of AI, and even a traditional telecommunications group that has used AI to supersede its global competitors.

Is It Safe To Invest In AI Right Now?

Just as the fierce trade war between the U.S. and China has begun to de-escalate, the U.S. dollar was pushed to its lowest level in three years on Thursday as Donald Trump’s 90-day pause on reciprocal tariffs fast approaches. The U.S. president confirmed he would reach out to global trading partners with new tariff rates in the coming weeks, reigniting fears over trade and geopolitical uncertainty.

But seasoned AI investors—and frequent readers of my columns—know now is not the time to panic. Many of the AI darling stocks of 2023 and 2024 have retreated sharply, but it's not because their fundamentals have changed.

As I discussed in a recent webinar on AI, tech stocks often lose value during market instability but tend to bounce back as economic confidence improves and market conditions stabilize. As heightened geopolitical tensions have led to a period of highly emotional investing and prices of stocks with strong underlying fundamentals recede, there has never been a better time to hunt for hidden gems in the market.

We’ve already seen that principle in action, as some of my ‘Biggest Losers’ (Strong Buy stocks experiencing major declines) have already begun to gain momentum amid strong financial performance. AI remains an excellent category for uncovering compelling investment opportunities, even as the tech sector comes under renewed pressure and the demand for hyperscale data centers, semiconductors, and other key AI components continues to surge.

But as AI becomes an increasingly ubiquitous part of our lives and the economy, how can you tell which companies make for truly good investments? Seeking Alpha’s Quant System can help you navigate the frothiness of the AI market and zero in on stocks with top-tier fundamentals, powerful momentum, and industry-leading valuations.

How I Picked the Top AI Stocks

Our data-driven Quant system leverages powerful computer processing and our proprietary algorithm to analyze thousands of stocks across a diverse range of sectors. It ranks them based on hundreds of metrics across value, growth, profitability, EPS revisions, and momentum, lending unique insights about their performance and long-term outlook only available to SA Premium and PRO subscribers.

To identify the top-performing stocks in the AI universe, I’ve used the Quant system to analyze securities from three of the largest AI ETFs—the Global X Robotics & Artificial Intelligence ETF (BOTZ), the Robo Global® Robotics and Automation Index ETF (ROBO, and the Global X Artificial Intelligence & Technology ETF—(AIQ) and selected five top-performing stocks to represent a diverse range of AI opportunities.

They include tech companies that sell AI-powered solutions and non-tech firms that employ AI to boost productivity, improve products, and streamline operations. My top five AI-leveraging stocks have all displayed strong momentum. They have returned an average of 26% YTD and have an average forward P/E ratio of 22.8.

Top 5 AI Stocks for June, by Quant Rating (as of 6/12/25)

1. Uber Technologies, Inc. (NYSE: UBER)

  • Market Capitalization: $179B

  • Sector: Industrials

  • Industry: Passenger Transportation

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 4 out of 618

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 4/8/25): 1 out of 8

Uber (UBER) is a top-performing Alpha Pick, having generated more than 128% to the portfolio since it was added in June, 2023.

The company is investing heavily in AI-powered autonomous vehicles, forming partnerships with AI tech firm Wayve and Nvidia (NVDA). “Generative AI will power the future of mobility, requiring both rich data and very powerful compute,” UBER CEO Dara Khosrowshahi said in January. “By working with NVIDIA, we are confident that we can help supercharge the timeline for safe and scalable autonomous driving solutions for the industry.”

In addition to top-tier Quant Factor Grades, Uber's profitability has exploded in recent months with Q1 EPS of $0.50 on $11.62B revenue, a 14.7% year-over year increase. It also projected Q1 2025 gross bookings growth of 17%-21%, suggesting that it will continue to grow revenue and expand margins going forward.

UBER Stock Quant Breakdown

Despite surging profitability, UBER’s valuation has remained impressively undervalued compared to its industrial peers. As SA analyst Kennedy Njagi recently wrote:

The stock is trading at a FWD PEG of 1x - an undervaluation of 42% against its sector median. Given its impressive growth profile, a combo of profitability and long-term FCFs, I think there is still a lot that the company has to offer.

In addition to its A- forward PEG, UBER also boasts an A+ trailing PEG of 0.02, a more than 98% discount to the wider sector. The rideshare group also exhibits excellent growth metrics, including ‘A+’ EPS GAAP growth of nearly 59%, a 520% difference from the 9.5% sector median. ‘A+’ profitability is lifted higher by return on common equity of 74% (a 501% difference vs. the sector) and $8.05B in cash from operations.

UBER also maintains strong momentum despite widespread market volatility: flat three-month price performance of 22.6% crushes the 4.6% sector median by nearly 390%.

Like UBER, my next selection has emerged as a global leader in its sector, offering dependable momentum and growth at a reasonable price.

2. Telefonaktiebolaget LM Ericsson (NASDAQ: ERIC)

  • Market Capitalization: $28.4B

  • Sector: Technology

  • Industry: Communications Equipment

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 14 out of 544

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 3 out of 41

Ericsson (ERIC) is a Swedish multinational networking and telecommunications group offering network infrastructure, hardware, and software, among other communications services. The company recently partnered with GOOG to develop its Ericsson On-Demand software-as-a-service (SaaS) platform, leveraging AI infrastructure and Google Kubernetes Engine to streamline operations.

The deal comes on the heels of a strong first quarter, with Q1 GAAP EPS of $0.13 and revenue of $5.83B, a 3.2% Y/Y increase. As SA analyst Daniel Jones wrote following the better-than-expected results in April:

Shares are attractively priced, and recent performance has been encouraging. The catalyst for growth for the company should propel it forward as time goes on as well. Given these factors, and despite certain headwinds such as global trade issues, inflation, and high interest rates, I believe that the enterprise warrants a soft "Buy" rating at this time.

ERIC was upgraded from a Hold to a Strong Buy by our Quant system in early April, a score that has continued to improve amid its improving growth and valuation.

ERIC Stock Quant Breakdown

As mentioned, ERIC’s valuation has recently grown even more attractive, improving from a C+ to a B over the past three months. Boosting that grade higher is its forward GAAP P/E of 15.84, a 47% discount from the broader technology sector.

The company’s growth grade has improved from a B+ to an A- during the same period, with A+ forward free cash flow per share growth of 89%, a 497% difference from the sector median, and operating cash flow growth of 63%, a 365% difference. Profitability remains strong with $4.53B in cash from operations, while the one-year price performance of 40.2%, a 7,759.5%, reflects its enduring momentum.

Similar to ERIC, my next selection has leveraged AI to unlock a new phase of growth that’s built to last thanks to powerful momentum among other high-quality Quant attributes.

3. Celestica Inc. (NYSE: CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $14.7B

  • Sector: Technology

  • Industry: Electronic Manufacturing Services

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 18 out of 544

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 2 out of 17

A two-time Alpha Pick, Celestica Inc. (CLS) is outshining volatile markets, surging 36% year-to-date and over 16% the past month after getting caught up in the initial tariff sell-off in March. The Toronto-based company, which manufactures critical electronic parts for data centers, is currently ranked the second-highest electronic manufacturing services stock due to massive hyperscaler demand.

CLS is also a top-performing Alpha Pick, returning more than 359% for the portfolio since it was initially selected on October 16, 2023. The group recently raised its revenue outlook for 2025 after stellar Q1 2025 earnings, with much of Wall Street aligned on its enduring star status.

As SA analyst Danil Sereda noted on Thursday regarding its financial performance:

Q1 FY 2025 saw a record 7.1% EBIT margin, with sales up 19.9% and EPS +39.5%. I see the CCS segment, fueled by hyperscaler AI demand (HPS +99% YoY), driving growth…Despite tariff risks and potential for priced-in gains, I believe Celestica's ongoing business transformation and strong AI-driven pipeline support my continued 'buy' rating for significant future returns.

CLS Stock’s Quant Breakdown

While CLS’s shares have grown more expensive in recent months on the back of soaring momentum, its forward P/E ratio of 29.49 reflects a fair price tag at a 1.4% discount from the sector. A trailing PEG of 0.95, a 1.55% discount, also helps justify its current price of $130.39 per share.

Growth remains strong, with forward EPS GAAP growth of 40%, a 266% difference to the 11% sector median among other impressive metrics.

Wall Street is similarly bullish on CLS with ten positive EPS revisions, ten positive revenue revisions, and only one negative revision in each category over the past 90 days.

Like CLS, this next stock continues to occupy a starring role in the AI economy.

4. Amazon, Inc. (NASDAQ: AMZN)

  • Market Capitalization: $2.26T

  • Sector: Consumer Discretionary

  • Industry: Broadline Retail

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 14 out of 477

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 3 out of 26

Amazon (AMZN) recently unveiled three new AI tools to deliver packages to customers faster, just the latest development in the tech giant’s AI-heavy strategy to expand its market cap and drive new business lines.

After pouring billions into artificial intelligence, its AI-powered Amazon Web Services and ad services businesses have emerged as powerful growth engines. Both have helped fuel strong Q1 2025 earnings results, including a 9% increase in net sales to $155.7B in comparison with $143.3B in Q1 2024. AWS in particular helped fuel massive growth, with sales in the segment increasing 17% Y/Y to $29.3B.

The unit recently unveiled a new quantum computing chip in addition to its partnerships with Anthropic and other AI innovators to compete with the likes of GOOG and MSFT.

AMZN Stock’s Quant Breakdown

AMZN’s forward PEG of 2.18 comes at a 32% premium to the wider consumer discretionary sector, a modest difference considering its gargantuan size. Its trailing PEG of 0.48, a 35.2% discount to the wider sector, also reflects a fair price tag.

As SA analyst Simple Investment Ideas recently noted on its valuation:

At Amazon's current valuation of $2.2 trillion and forward P/E of 33, the company is still relatively cheap given its ability to integrate state-of-the-art AI into its increasingly strong ecosystem.

AMZN registers an A- Growth Grade overall, pushed higher by forward EPS diluted growth of 35.8%, a 512% difference from the sector median. Profitability remains high, with a massive $113.9B cash in operations and a 14.3% capex to sales ratio (a 397% difference to the sector), boosting its A+ score.

AMZN’s B momentum grade has demonstrated improvement in recent months, with a three-month price performance of 8.45% coming in at an 876.8% difference to the 0.86% sector median. It has also received 35 up EPS revisions and 16 down revisions in the last 90 days.

Similar to AMZN, my next selection has navigated intense pressure from tariffs and inflation by leveraging AI to build powerful new revenue streams.

5. Seagate Technology Holdings (NASDAQ: STX)

  • Market Capitalization: $26.8B

  • Sector: Technology

  • Industry: Technology Hardware, Storage and Peripherals Stocks

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 22 out of 544

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 6/12/25): 22 out of 544

Seagate Technology Holdings (STX) is an Ireland-based computer hardware manufacturer, such as hard disk drives and storage systems. The company put some analysts’ nerves to rest regarding tariffs and demand with better-than-expected earnings results in April. As SA analyst Gary Alexander noted at the time:

Seagate's strong Q3 earnings and minimal tariff impact have driven a 10% post-earnings stock surge, recouping all YTD losses and pushing shares up for the year. The company benefits from robust AI-driven datacenter demand, leading to significant y/y growth and larger storage module shipments.

STX Stock’s Quant Breakdown

STX touts an excellent valuation grade, with an ‘A+’ forward PEG of 0.14—a 92% discount to the sector. Its forward GAAP P/E of 19.14 is also strong at a 36% discount to the sector.

Forward EPS diluted growth of 87%, a 2,533% difference from the sector median, is one of many impressive metrics contributing to its ‘A+’ growth grade. Momentum has improved from a C+ to as high as an A- in recent months, with three-month price performance of 45.4% coming at a 462% difference to the 8% sector median.

Like the other four selections in this piece, STX’s resilient momentum leaves it in a strong position to outlast any temporary volatility that arises from the next round of tariff negotiations between the Trump administration and its global trade partners.

The Big Takeaway

As the U.S. government’s 90-day pause on retaliatory tariffs nears its close, don’t be surprised when investors begin to shed their holdings left and right. Rather than being one of them, consider adding resilient stocks with rock-solid momentum such as the five AI-leveraging stocks mentioned in this piece. Semiconductors, data centers, and other AI megatrends have proven their ability to outlast market swings triggered by tariffs and inflation.

My top AI picks for June offer market-crushing momentum and competitive valuations. Retaining a diverse collection of AI stocks with strong fundamentals is a great way to embrace long-term investment trends over short-term volatility.

For additional stock recommendations like these, our new PRO Quant Portfolio offers a diverse and carefully curated blend of 30 stocks spanning various geographies and market capitalizations. You can find more information on the game-changing product here.

Alternatively, consider joining our Alpha Picks community to receive two new Quant Strong Buy portfolio additions each month, with real-time sell alerts when ratings shift and detailed performance analysis.

data.title: Corcept: Remains A Strong Buy On Recent Weakness And Aggressive Sales Force Hiring
data.description:
Corcept Therapeutics (NASDAQ:CORT) remains a strong buy as the recent weakness was created by huge put options trading, aggressively expanding its sales force, maintained 2025 and long-term revenue guidance and will be submitting a New Drug Application by September for an entirely different disease than what the company is known for.

Corcept’s fundamental medications help lessen the impact of the hormone Cortisol, known as the stress hormone, and is one of about 50 hormones in a human body. From the work the company has done to identify over 1,000 compounds it appears that mitigating cortisol can have positive effects on a wide range of serious diseases.

Weakness last week created by huge put options trading

On Monday last week, June 2, Corcept’s stock fell $5.88 from $77.56 to $71.68 with 6.1 million shares traded when about 1.7 million typically are. It dropped $2.17 or 3.0% on Tuesday to $69.51. Last week saw over 16,000 put options created or the equivalent of 1.6 million shares. The market makers who had to create them probably sold some number of shares to limit their exposure, creating downside pressure on the stock. This could have created a lot of downside stress on the shares.

While the shares have recovered 20% of the decrease over the past three days, they are still down 9.0% or $6.32 since Friday, May 30.

March quarter results fell way short of expectations

While Corcept generated $157 million of revenue in the March quarter, it only increased 7% year-over-year and missed expectations by $20 million. The company attributed the miss to problems at its single-source pharmacy (it has limited itself to one so that very tight prescription control is maintained due to the side effect of terminating pregnancies). The March quarter results in many ways mirrored the December quarter revenue shortfall as I previously wrote. While the company made its revenue guidance of $675 million for the full year, revenue was flat quarter-over-quarter versus the $11 million plus results the previous four quarters.

However, management stated the problem has been resolved with record tablets dispensed in March and April. Sean Maduck, Corcept’s President Endocrinology, said on the conference call “Our first quarter results were also affected by a greater portion of our business transitioning from branded Korlym to our authorized generic, which has a lower net price. As a result, our average price per tablet decreased by 13% relative to the prior year. We expect that the transition to authorized generic tablets will continue to grow this year, but that this decrement in price will be overwhelmed by an increase in the number of tablets shipped.” This is the first indication that Teva’s generic version has created price pressure on Corcept. However, over half of Corcept’s tablet sales are at the lower price so it doesn’t appear that the switch to generic versions will have a large impact.

EPS was a reported $0.17 vs. expectations of $0.14. However, there was a tax benefit of $10.9 million that added $0.10 to the bottom line. Therefore, adjusted EPS of $0.07 missed, which would be expected when revenue came up so short. Every $10 million in revenue would have added about $0.07 in EPS.

One positive on the income statement was research and development expenses falling almost $10 million from the December quarter. This verifies that the spike of $11 million in the December quarter was due to an inventory buildup of Relacorilant as the cost had to be expensed since it is not an approved medication, yet.

Management said single source pharmacy was overwhelmed but the problem is fixed

On the conference call Maduck said, “Our Q1 financial results do not reflect the tremendous patient growth our business is experiencing or what we expect for the rest of this year. Our first quarter results, specifically in January and February, were affected by insufficient capacity at our pharmacy vendor. As I said on our last call, the rapid growth in our business in the second half of 2024 overwhelmed the pharmacy's operational capabilities, and these challenges persisted into this year. That said, we have seen a substantial improvement in pharmacy operations in March and April. This has translated to a record number of tablets dispensed in March and April.” In a recent sell-side report it confirmed that the pharmacy issue was resolved in March and business has returned to normal.

In response to a question about meeting the potential higher demand for Relacorilant Maduck said, “there is the potential for a broader distribution network for that product, and that's something that we're actively exploring now.” Management got burned by not planning for a single point of failure problem and I suspect it will have at least one more, if not a handful of, specialized pharmacies that can supply Relacorilant.

2025 and long-term revenue guidance maintained

Corcept reiterated its 2025 revenue guidance of $900 to $950 million or growth of 33% to 41%, respectively, year-over-year. However, due to only increasing 7% in the first quarter the growth rates from April to December will need to be 41% to 50% year-over-year.

On the conference call Maduck, said “we expect the growth to continue through the second quarter and the rest of the year and actually accelerate in the half of the second half of the year.” Joe Belanoff, Corcept’s CEO, added, “we're not expecting an instant bolus. We just think the growth will accelerate through the entire year and then into the next year.” While these are very high growth projections, based on comments about the number of new patients, increased testing and aggressive salesforce hiring, they could be achieved.

This was the second quarter that management gave the same long-term revenue guidance of $3 to $5 billion in 3 to 5 years from the $900 to $950 million in 2025. This translates to 28% revenue growth of $3 billion in 2030 to 65% compounded growth for $5 billion in 2028. These revenue outlooks are only based on sales for hypercortisolism and does not include any benefit from additional medications.

June quarter results will be critical

While every quarter is the most important one before it is reported as a tech CFO once told me, I think the upcoming June quarter does fall in that category as there are very steep quarter-to-quarter revenue increases needed to get to $900 million in revenue this year.

The sell-side analysts estimates are:

March quarter: $157 million actual result

June quarter: $199 million, up $42 million from March or 21% growth year-over-year

Sept. quarter: $248 million, up $49 million from June or 36% year-over-year

Dec. quarter: $295 million, up $47 million from September or 62% year-over-year

Total: $899 million or 33% year-over-year

3 Reasons Corcept can make its aggressive revenue guidance

While analysts and outside observers can make projections, management of any company has much more information about its business and outlook. That being said, the combination of these three reasons are how Corcept can make its revenue guidance.

#1 Increased testing for hypercortisolism

Corcept’s findings from its CATALYST trial showed that 23.8% of hard-to-treat diabetics (those with an A1c level of 7.5 or higher) have hypercortisolism and that by taking Korlym it lowered A1c levels by 1.47%. These results became known last year which led to increased testing and subsequent very large growth in prescriptions for Korlym. It led to my analysis that there could be over 1 million people with hard-to-treat diabetes that Corcept’s medications could help.

With additional results to be presented at the American Diabetes Associations Scientific Sessions in two weeks and the publication of them in a few months, this should provide another boost to awareness and testing. On the conference call Maduck said, “our first quarter enrollments (meaning prescriptions) were almost double what they were in the first quarter of last year. And we've seen that continue on (conference call was on May 5).”

#2 Corcept is hiring salespeople aggressively

On the conference call Maduck said, “We currently have 125 clinical specialists, up from 60 at the beginning of 2024, and our plan is to have 175 in place before year-end in preparation for Relacorilant’s launch.”

I have been following Corcept’s job openings since February 28 this year. Over the past 14 weeks the company has hired 45 Endocrinology salespeople with 40 open positions still on its website, 10 Regional field sales managers for Endocrinology and in early May it listed 49 Oncology Account Managers positions. Note that since the conference call Corcept now has approximately 145 Endocrinology salespeople and has been hiring about 3 per week since the end of February.

To have such an aggressive hiring plan, Management must feel very confident about the increased testing, driven by educating doctors, on the higher than previously thought hypercortisolism in hard-to-treat diabetics and the need to introduce Corcept to oncology doctors.

#3 Major backlog of Korlym prescriptions

Based on my analysis, Corcept’s pharmacy shipped about the same number of tablets in the March quarter as the September and December quarters last year. This takes the 13.3% price pressure in the March quarter into account that was disclosed on the conference call and 10-Q (used on a quarterly basis vs. yearly stated on the conference call). It would indicate that the pharmacy had flat tablet shipments for three quarters in a row because revenue was flat from the September to December quarters and fell 13.6% from the December to March quarters.

This leads to the thought that there must be a major backlog of prescriptions to be filled as the company has said a record number of prescriptions have been written in the past few quarters and it wasn’t until the month of March that had record shipments.

Additional reasons Corcept will be successful

Corcept announced positive results for its Phase 3 ROSELLA trial of Relacorilant in Patients with Platinum-Resistant Ovarian Cancer in March. The company plans to submit a New Drug Application in the United States by September and a Marketing Authorization Application in Europe shortly thereafter.

A major reason this is so important is that Corcept now has a medication that treats cancer vs. the current focus on endocrinology and hypercortisolism. It demonstrates that the company could create a totally different revenue stream and while it will require other trials to be successful, with over 1,000 compounds it appears that the odds are in its favor.

Oncology is the second (Endocrinology is the first) major platform Corcept is having success. The other two that are being tested are Neurology and Liver Disease.

To dive deeper into Corcept’s business and outlook check out its Investor Presentation.

Assuming Relacorilant is approved by the end of the year, it should negate the threat from generics, additional doctors should be willing to prescribe it since it doesn’t terminate pregnancies as Korlym does, has fewer side effects than Korlym and should increase retention rates due to the fewer side effects. The company could also reach out to patients that stopped taking Korlym due to its side effects.

Corcept generates very strong cash flows and has no debt. It currently has $571 million in cash and investments and depending on how much it spends on share buybacks (it spent $43 million in the March quarter at an average price of $54.84), it could have $1 billion by the end of 2026, or 13% of its current market cap.

Major risks revolve around Relacorilant being approved and Teva being successful with its lawsuits or generic version

There are two major risks to the shares both revolving around Relacorilant, Korlym’s follow on drug, not being approved by the FDA as it is always an unknown when and if a new drug will be given the thumps up. At least in this instance Corcept seems to have built a very strong case for the medication. The New Drug Application, or NDA, is based on the positive results from four trials or studies, one of which had patients on Relacorilant for up to six years. The NDA was filed on December 30 last year and on March 3 this year the FDA accepted the application. Note that this is the normal process for the FDA to first take about two months to accept the application and Relacorilant has a target date of December 30 this year for it to be approved or not.

The second biggest risk is Teva Pharmaceutical’s litigation to bring a generic version of Corcept’s Korlym medication to market. Corcept first sued Teva in 2018 for patent infringement and the latest ruling in early January 2024 was ruled in Teva’s favor, which Corcept is appealing. Teva also sued Corcept in June last year accusing the company of monopolizing the market for mifepristone-based Korlym. There is an appellate court hearing scheduled for July 7 with a decision sometime in the fourth quarter.

Besides having Relacorilant approved by the FDA, since it looks to be more effective than Corcept’s current Korlym, the new medication could largely shutout Teva from a large portion of the Cushing Syndrome market.

It does appear that Teva’s generic is only having minor impact to Corcept. While Corcept has seen over half of its patients move to its own generic, the 13% price differential appears to being overwhelmed by increased prescriptions and Corcept continues to provide the same level of support, no matter which version the patient it is taking.

Corcept’s shares are in a positive trend

Corcept’s price chart has been in a very positive uptrend since mid-2024 as the news about its addressable patient base grew over 100 times from 10,000 patients to over 1 million. From a technical analysis perspective, as long as it remains above its 150-day moving average the shares should continue their climb.

Corcept’s stock was slightly overbought before the drop last week with an RSI or Relative Strength Index of about 60. The RSI fell on Monday to around 43 and has moved back to 47.4, or essentially a neutral level.

The stock falling below its 50-day moving average could create some resistance for it to rise above $72.45 in the short-term. On Tuesday it traded above the 50-day but closed below it.

Build a position to take advantage of Corcept’s multiple strengths

While Corcept’s shares have already increased over 40% year-to-date, I believe they can move higher over the next year and especially over a two-to-four-year timeframe. This is based on the potential patient population of a million or more for hard-to-treat diabetes, additional revenue streams from oncology, potential from neurology and liver disease medications and being acquired from one of the large pharmaceutical companies. Another game changer would be positive results from its MOMENTUM trial for hard-to-treat hypertension (high blood pressure) late this year or early 2026.

Plan for volatility around the Teva lawsuit hearing on July 7, second quarter results being announced in late July/early August, the NDA filing for platinum-resistant ovarian cancer sometime in the September quarter, late October/early November for third quarter results, potential results from its MOMENTUM trial and finally the FDA decision on Relacorilant, hopefully no later than the announced December 30, if not a bit earlier.

data.title: HCI: Too Good To Ignore, But Wait For Risk To Fade
data.description:
Introduction

HCI Group (NYSE:HCI) is a Tampa-based P&C insurance company operating primarily in the volatile and high-risk hurricane market of Florida. Despite operating in the high-risk market, HCI has demonstrated steady performance and remarkable investor returns over the last many years.

HCI recently delivered quite remarkable improvement in its results over the last few years, driven by stabilization of the Florida P&C market owing to favourable legislative measures. This improvement was duly noted by investors, leading to a sharp appreciation of HCI’s stock price

In this article, I will try to provide an answer to whether HCI is still a buy even after this run-up, and explain key drivers and potential risks relative to current valuations.

Company Overview

As already mentioned in the introduction, HCI is a Florida-based company primarily in the Florida P&C insurance market. However, it also acts as a holding company for various businesses serving in P&C insurance, information technology, insurance management, and the real estate market. Its core insurance business is operated under two different subsidiaries named Homeowners Choice Property & Casualty Insurance Company (HCPCI), TypTap Insurance Company. Apart from these subsidiaries, HCI also acts as a management company for two reciprocal exchanges named Tailrow Reciprocal Exchange and Condo Owners Reciprocal Exchange (CORE) under the attorney-in-fact structure, where the company gets a stable fee-based revenue from these companies. The company also has a captive reinsurance subsidiary named Claddaugh Casualty Insurance Company Ltd.

HCI technology arm, Exzeo Group, is quite aligned with the primary business of Insurance, providing solutions based on advanced algorithms and data analytics to calculate risk metrics for a given insurance policy and enable the HCI to price the policy accordingly. This type of technology provides a competitive advantage to HCI’s insurance business in terms of risk assessment and pricing, enhancing risk-adjusted return for the company. Apart from these businesses, the company also has a less significant real estate operation in Florida for investment purposes.

Continues to deliver solid performance

Back in May, HCI reported upbeat results, beating the consensus estimates on both bottom and topline front. The company reported $300 million gross premiums earned, a growth of ~17% YoY, driven by encouraging market sentiment and assumption of policies worth $35 million from Citizen Insurance Corporation. While the growth rate might look like it has slowed down considerably from 38% in 4Q24 to 17% now, it is primarily due to the tough comparison that the company has started to witness from 1Q25. The growth on the topline was well reflected in the bottom line, which grew ~40% YoY to $5.35, improving primarily due to better underwriting.

The loss ratio, which is a metric to gauge underwriting efficiency, was exceptionally low, a little lower than 20%, primarily due to lower claims. Usually, insurers witness lower claim volume 4-6 months after a hurricane. Hence, the loss ratio was exceptionally lower this time. If we were to normalize this loss ratio should have fallen somewhere ~24-25%, which in itself is quite commendable. Similarly, the net combined ratio was also remarkable, reaching to 56% level from 67% in 1Q24, driven by lower loss ratios and policy assumptions from Citizen Insurance Corp. Compared to other P&C insurance peers operating in Florida, HCI’s combined ratio was the best, which showcases its industry-leading underwriting standards.

Company

Q1 2025 Combined Ratio

HCI Group (HCI)

56%

American Coastal (ACIC)

65%

Heritage Insurance Holdings (HRTG)

84.5%

Universal Insurance Holdings (UVE)

95.0%

Florida P&C market tailwinds

The Florida P&C market was going through a pretty rough period over the last many years due to frequent catastrophic events, excessive litigation, and fraud, the latter being the cause for many insurance companies to leave the state. As a result, the Florida P&C market has become unprofitable for many insurers, with 2024 being the first profitable year in close to eight years.

The primary reason for this turnaround has been the major legislative reform that the state of Florida has undergone to curb excessive litigation and fraud. Particularly, the Senate Bill 2A (SB-2A) of December 2022 and House Bill 293 (effective from May 2024) have played an important role in the market rebound.

The benefits of these bills can be witnessed in various Florida-based P&C insurance companies. For instance, HCI itself has reported a swift increase in its return ratio and book value over the course of several quarters.

Exzeo spin-off to unlock value

Currently, the company operates Exzeo as a separate business unit providing technological assistance for the HCI group’s Insurance arms for effective underwriting. The business unit has technologies like SAMS, Harmony, ClaimColony, and AtlasViewer, which provide data-driven insights for effective underwriting.

Currently, these technologies support ~$1.2 billion worth of premiums within the HCI group. However, this is just a small fraction of the overall P&C insurance market. These technologies are scalable and can offer services to various other insurers. Hence, to avoid any potential conflict of interest, HCI is planning for a tax-free spin-off of this technology division by the end of 2025.

As far as financials are concerned, the Exzeo platform had ~$52 million in revenue and $24 million in pretax income for 1Q25. Given its profitability, the company is ripe to operate and succeed independently. Moreover, as an independent company, it should also fetch a better valuation multiple as compared to the HCI group.

Valuation

As per Seeking Alpha's quant rating, HCI fetches a B grade for valuation. The grade indicates that the company is priced at a lucrative valuation. While the grade is arrived at by taking various ratios into consideration, I like to value financial stocks by the Price to book ratio in combination with ROE.

HCI has a price-to-book ratio of ~3.19, which is quite high given the sector median of 1.2x but close to its own 5Y average. I believe this higher P/B ratio is justified given HCI’s extraordinary ROE, which currently stands at ~27%. I believe this level of ROE to sustain for some time, given the underlying tailwind in the industry. Moreover, a potential value unlocking from the spin-off of Exzeo sweetens the deal even more.

Risk

HCI derives much of its insurance business from the Florida market, which is often the worst hit state from the Atlantic Hurricanes. Leading meteorological departments like the Colorado Meterological department are anticipating an above normal hurricane season this year, with the hurricane activities being 125% of the average from 1991-2020. For comparison, the hurricane activity during 2024, which caused ~$120 billion worth of damage in southeastern states, was 130% above average.

Conclusion

Given HCI's continued fundamental performance, buoyant Florida P&C market, and potential of value unlocking from the Exzeo spin-off, I am drawn to issue a buy rating on the stock at current valuation. However, given the risk of an above-average hurricane season this year, I would like this risk to pass before issuing a buy rating. Until then, I have a hold rating on the stock.

data.title: SCHD's Dual Potential For Income And Growth
data.description:
In today's investment environment, finding the right balance between consistent income and capital growth continues to be a priority for many standard investors. I believe the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) is the most compelling option that effectively addresses both goals. In this comprehensive analysis, I'll explain why SCHD offers a balanced approach for investors looking for steady income while maintaining growth potential, and I'll explain why I'm a "buy."

A Foundation of Quality Stock Selection

SCHD focuses on companies that demonstrate fundamental strength relative to their peers. SCHD's selection of quality stocks sets SCHD apart from most dividend-focused ETFs that prioritize yield alone. Methodologically, SCHD selects companies with a proven 10-year track record of paying dividends, which adds to the fund's credibility.

Instead of simply selecting high-yielding stocks, SCHD uses fundamental metrics such as cash flow-to-debt ratios, return on equity (ROE), dividend yield and dividend growth rates to identify companies with sustainable dividend practices. This way, the portfolio both seeks to secure potential long-term value growth while significantly reducing the risk of dividend cuts.

Income Reliability and Growth

Since its IPO in 2011, SCHD provided its investors with an increasing dividend yield every year. In fact, except for 2023, dividend per share distributed by SCHD increased much more than inflation every year. SCHD's 5-year dividend growth CAGR is 6.77% and if dividends continue to grow at this CAGR for the next 5 years, the dividend per share it will distribute in 2030 will be $1.44.

Even though dividend growth fell below 10% if this trend continues, linear regression calculation also gives a dividend target of $1.56 for 2030. So I'm targeting SCHD's average dividend per share to be around $1.5 in 2030.

Even though SCHD's dividend yield was on a downtrend before 2021 and fell from 4.25% to 3.20%, it broke this trend by rising above 3.75% after 2021. I calculate that the average dividend yield was around 3.69% since 2012. If SCHD maintains this average dividend yield in the coming years, we can expect the share price to be around $40 in 2030.

Portfolio Construction with Dual Objectives

The number of assets constituting SCHD's portfolio will be 103 as of mid-2025. It is good that the assets in the portfolio are both from a variety of sectors and consist of quality dividend-paying stocks. Almost 20% of the portfolio is consumer defensive and almost 20% is energy stocks. Nearly 15% is healthcare stocks, while technology and industrial stocks cover 11%. Financial sector stocks cover only 9% of the portfolio.

The fund's largest positions include companies with strong competitive advantages such as Texas Instruments (TXN) (4.26%), The Coca-Cola Company (KO) (4.25%), Verizon Communications (VZ) (4.25%) and ConocoPhillips (COP) (4.23%).

Considering this, it can be clearly said that SCHD is an ETF consisting of both growth-oriented and traditional defensive companies, rather than the high-volatility Nasdaq technology ETF. So I can say that it is an ETF that investors who expect rapid and high capital growth will not be happy with.

SCHD limits individual security allocations to 4% and sectors to 25%. This is an approach that I really like, which prevents excessive exposure to a single company or sector. So I think SCHD is one of the ETFs that managed to minimize possible market risks. In addition to that, the portfolio is rebalanced every three months with an annual composition review in order to adapt to changing market conditions.

Track Record

SCHD has a performance history that supports this dual structure. SCHD provided a return of nearly 8% in 2024 and provided positive returns in 11 of the 14 years since its IPO. SCHD, which closed 6 of the last 10 years with a positive return, brought an average of 14.36% in these years. In the 4 years it closed with a loss, an average loss of 6.20% was recorded.

When normalized for inflation, the positive return rate it has provided in the last 10 years is 13.29% on average, while the loss rate decreases and the loss rate increases to 7.48%. So I can say that it is a fund that is in real profit.

The ETF has grown significantly in investor acceptance, and assets under management reached approximately $70 billion by 2025. With an average daily trading volume of approximately 14.3 million shares, this scale also provides liquidity advantages.

Top 10 Stocks

Valuation

I use the forward P/E method to evaluate 10 stocks that cover 41% of the shares held by SCHD. According to this method, 7 out of 10 stocks appear to be much cheaper than the SP500 average in terms of forward P/E. While 2 are almost at the SP500 average, only TXN is much more expensive than the index.

The stocks that are at the index average and more expensive than the index are also somewhat more expensive than their own sectors, while only 5 of the stocks that are cheaper than the index appear to be significantly cheaper than their own sectors.

Forward P/E analysis shows me that 5 stocks that are cheaper than the index and the sector have significant upside potential, while stocks that are cheaper than the index but more expensive than the sector also give the impression that they have at least some upside potential. I don't see any stocks with downside risk other than TXN. So SCHD has more bullish potential in general.

Profitability

Of the 10 stocks that SCHD holds the most, 2 have ROEs above 100% and 7 have ROEs above 10%. In addition to that, the cost of equity of all of these stocks is below 10%. So I can say that 9 out of 10 companies actively create shareholder value.

When I look at the ROICs of the 10 stocks, I see that all of them have ROICs above 7%. The maximum ROIC is in MO, with 51.12%. Their WACCs are generally between 4.9% and 11%. The economic profitability of all stocks except CVX is positive. So, 9 out of 10 companies actively creating value.

Risks and Limitations

While I consider SCHD as a strong choice for dividend-focused investors, there are significant risks. One of the primary risks relates to sector concentration.

At the moment, the largest part of SCHD's portfolio is in the energy sector. This makes SCHD vulnerable to fluctuations in oil prices and global energy demand. This effect is particularly pronounced during periods of trade tensions or when OPEC adjusts production levels. So it is important to be careful.

Additionally, SCHD has a relatively low technology sector allocation. Because of that, investors often miss out on the growth opportunities provided by these companies. So this lack of technology increases the risk of falling behind in broad market rallies and expose investors who expect capital growth to opportunity costs.

Charting

SCHD, continuing its uptrend since it was launched on the monthly timeframe, supported by the 22-month Hodrick-Prescott filter. Even though it affected by general market trends since the beginning of 2025 and declined to $23, it managed to hold on the filter again. As long as it remains on the HP filter, I determine that SCHD positively diverged from the HP filter by approximately 25% in every bull run. So, I can say that the target is around $32.70 in the new bull run scenario.

When I go down to the weekly timeframe, I see that SCHD is currently at the $27 price action resistance level. In addition, I think that SCHD, which has been in a downtrend since November, will end this downtrend if it breaks this price action support level. The biggest reason for this is that momentum was increasing positively since April, as can be seen by the MACD indicator.

As a result of that, I prefer to stay on the "buy" side in the long term, as I think SCHD will break the $27 resistance one way or another.

Conclusion

As a result of that, SCHD is a pretty good choice for investors who are looking for a balanced approach between consistent income and capital growth. Disciplined stock selections that show fundamental strength, dividend reliability and sector diversification make the portfolio to have sustainable long-term returns while minimizing risk.

While SCHD's limited technology exposure caused it to lag behind in broad tech-focused rallies, its solid track record, reliable dividend growth and positive valuation metrics across its underlying assets provide a strong case for inflation-adjusted earnings. When I look at its current technical setup, I believe SCHD remains a "buy" for investors prioritizing both income and conservative growth in today's investment environment.

Underlying Indexes

IYY tracks the Dow Jones U.S. Index, defined as the top 95% of U.S.-traded stocks based on a float-adjusted market capitalization, with certain liquidity requirements. The index is reconstituted annually in September, with quarterly updates including weight rebalancing.

IWB tracks the Russell 1000® Index, defined as “a subset of the Russell 3000® Index that includes approximately 1,000 of the largest companies in the US equity universe”. The Russell 3000 itself is designed to represent approximately 98% of the investable US equity market. The Russell 1000 is reconstituted annually in June, and will be reconstituted semi-annually starting in 2026.

Although these definitions differ, they result in almost identical portfolios, as we will see in the next section.

Portfolios

data.title: WIW: Inflation Bond CEF, 8.7% Distribution, -11.5% Discount To NAV
data.description:
I rate Western Asset Infl-Linked Opps & Inc Fd (NYSE:WIW) a Buy, for income focused investors who are looking for monthly income with a yield above 8%. This closed-end fund comprised of inflation-protected taxable bonds is currently trading at a discount of -11.47% to net asset value (NAV), making it very attractive for monthly income.

While the fear of inflation has all but gone away, the first quarter results of this fund were very positive. Given its high investment grade rating and discount to NAV, the fund could be bought just based on the high yield vs. the focus on inflation protection, which is part of its name. Analysts forecast at least one, maybe two rate cuts at the end of the year, which would benefit this fund.

The most important factors that influence my opinion of the CEF are:

  • Trades at an -11.47% discount to NAV
  • Pays a fixed monthly dividend of $0.0625, yielding 8.74%, going ex-dividend on June 23rd.
  • Leverage ratio of 30.18% provides additional income.
  • Low turnover ratio of 13%
  • Expected rate cuts are good for a fund like this.

Seeking Alpha Quant doesn’t rate the fund.

Western Asset Management – A Subsidiary of Franklin Templeton

Based in Pasadena, California, Western Asset is a global fixed income company with over $245.5 billion in AUM. In 1986 Western Asset was acquired by Legg Mason, who was later acquired by Franklin Templeton in 2020, making Western Asset a subsidiary of Franklin Templeton.

Founded in 1971, the firm is known for team management and proprietary research, supported by robust risk management and a long-term fundamental value approach. Each group of sector specialists utilizes their expertise in bottom-up analysis of each portfolio sector.

Inflation Linked Opportunities & Income Fund

With an inception date of February 27, 2004, the primary investment objective of the fund is to provide current income. Capital appreciation, when consistent with current income, is a secondary investment objective. Under normal market conditions and at the time of purchase, the Fund will invest at least 80% of its total managed assets in inflation-linked securities.

Dividend Policy - The Fund intends to make regular monthly distributions to common shareholders at a fixed rate per common share, which rate may be adjusted from time to time by the Fund’s Board of Trustees. This policy has no impact on the Fund’s investment strategy and may reduce the Fund’s NAV. The Fund’s investment adviser believes the policy helps maintain the Fund’s competitiveness and may benefit the Fund’s market price and premium/discount to the Fund’s NAV.

As shown in the chart below, the fund is actually comprised of many different sectors. Of course, the largest sector is inflation-linked bonds at 82.2%. The remaining 17% are comprised of emerging market debt, investment grade credit, non-agency mortgage-backed securities and commercial mortgage-backed securities.

The credit quality of the fund is investment grade, with 89% of the bond having a credit rating of “A” or better.

Q1 2025 Results

For the first quarter ending March 31, 2025, the fund returned 5.34% at NAV, beating the benchmarks such as Bloomberg U.S. Govt Inflation Linked 1-10 years at 3.93%.

  • Emerging markets exposures were additive for returns.
  • Corporate credit exposures in total boosted results.

Overall, the first quarter performed very well in my view. Since the fund is only 82% inflation linked, the portfolio manager can beat the benchmarks with higher yielding debt as is found with emerging markets, and corporations over the lower yielding Treasuries.

Performance To Date

$10,000 invested in WIW over the past 10 years would be worth $15,000 today, performing well above sector benchmarks such as Vanguard Short Term Inflation Protection Securities Index ETF (VTIP) which grew to $13,170, or iShares TIPS Bond ETF (TIP) at $12,750 or Vanguard Total Bond Index (BND) at $11,820.

Valuation

Since inception in 2004, the CEF has shown a discount to NAV of about 11%. The chart below shows how, just after it launched, like many closed end funds, the price dropped to a discount, but this one remained at a steep discount, currently reading -11.46%.

In a comparison to peers, WIW stands out for having the highest distribution yield and 5-year dividend growth rate. Surprisingly, it has a lower turnover rate than two other funds focused on TIPS – Treasury Inflation Protected Securities.

iShares TIPS Bond ETF (TIP) – although not a direct competitor to WIW, the TIPS focused fund has a much lower dividend yield of 2.5% and a much lower price drop over the past 10 years, as interest rates rose.

Western Asset Inflation-Linked Income Fund (WIA) – this is another fund to consider from the same fund family. WIA has a 7.4% dividend yield but a much larger price drop over the past 5 years.

Goldman Sachs Access Inflation Protected USD Bond ETF (GTIP) – this fund is tiny for the industry earning only 3.6% yield. Its 5-year dividend growth of 9.8% is healthy, with only 31 holdings and a surprising 39% turnover rate.

Outlook

In May, the Consumer Price Index (CPI) rose 0.1 percent to 2.4% over the last 12 months, which is approaching the Fed’s Target of 2%. While the Fed would love to claim victory over inflation, the President Trump tariffs haven’t yet started to appear in the CPI data. In the next few months, economists will be watching this carefully, as the rise of inflation will put a damper on the next wave of expected Fed rate cuts in the 4th quarter.

In my opinion, the tariff effect will raise inflation modestly but is projected to flatten out over time, as the tariffs are negotiated away and manufacturers find it harder to raise prices. The portfolio manager may find higher yielding opportunities in the emerging market space if rates continue to fall in the U.S.

The next ex-dividend date is June 23, 2025, with a fixed distribution of $0.0625 announced for this and the next two months.

Risks To Rating

Fixed income securities are subject to interest rate, credit, and reinvestment risks. This fund also uses leverage which has the effect of boosting returns when the fund is going in the right direction, or lowering returns when factors are working against the fund.

Bottom Line

I rate Western Asset Inflation Linked Opportunities & Income Fund – WIW a Buy for income investors interested in monthly income with a distribution rate of 8.74%. The fund trades at an 11.5% discount to NAV, which has been in effect since its inception in 2004. The fund pays monthly distributions and is made up of over 80% inflation protected securities, with the remaining 20% in emerging markets and corporates, which increase the yield. The fund has a leverage ratio of 30% which magnifies gains but has the ability to extend losses as well. The risks to a fund like this are increasing interest rates. In the current macroeconomic environment, there is a risk to rising inflation due to tariffs, however analysts expect the inflation to be temporary and the Fed to cut later in the year, which would be a positive catalyst to the fund.

data.title: Inflation Doubts To Keep The Fed On Hold Until December
data.description:
By James Knightley & Padhraic Garvey, CFA

Updated Fed forecasts could suggest just one cut in 2025

The Federal Reserve is widely expected to leave monetary policy unchanged on 18 June. Central Bank officials have suggested they will be patient in assessing the impact of the President’s tariff policy on growth and inflation, implying little prospect of a rate change before September. The key thing to watch will be updated Fed forecasts and whether they continue to point to 50bp of rate cuts both this year and next. Given this remains in line with market pricing we sense they will choose to stick with them, but the risk is that switches to 25bp for this year and 75bp for next given uncertainty on how quickly inflation will slow after tariffs hit.

ING's expectations for Federal Reserve projections

Growth risks point to further rate cuts over time

While the 'Liberation Day' tariffs have been scaled back in the wake of some significant market angst in April there is concern that some damage has resulted and there continues to be the risk of occasional flare-ups in tensions.

Steep falls in consumer confidence suggest downside risk to consumer spending growth as households worry that tariff-induced price spikes will squeeze spending power at a time when perceptions of the jobs market are deteriorating. Meanwhile, a lack of clarity on the trading environment means there is the potential for corporates to delay decisions on hiring and investment. The latest Beige Book, which heavily influences Fed monetary policy, was downbeat on the growth story. It warned a majority of Fed Districts are reporting “slight to moderate declines in activity” while “comments about uncertainty delaying hiring were widespread”. With the Fed funds rate still up at 4.5% versus their 3% forecast for where they see the policy rate settling over time, this indicates that a case is building for renewed interest rate cuts.

But inflation challenges mean it could be delayed

However, tariffs do risk higher goods prices and this is likely to keep the Fed cautious. The Beige Book commented that “there were widespread reports of contacts expecting costs and prices to rise at a faster rate going forward. A few Districts described these expected cost increases as strong, significant, or substantial”. So while recent benign inflation prints are welcome, we think investors should be braced for the month-on-month rates to pick up to 0.4% or even 0.5% from July onwards. Tariff-induced price hikes could be amplified further if the spike in energy prices in the wake of the Israeli attacks on Iran is sustained.

We therefore think September is too soon for the Fed to be comfortable cutting interest rates. We will only have data for July and August by that point, and we don’t think there will be enough evidence of labour market stress to offset the fears that near-term elevate inflation readings could persist.

Housing costs could help to offset the impact of tariffs

A December cut, potentially of 50bp, is our call

Instead, we think that December is the more likely start point for the Fed to start cutting interest rates. We see tariffs and energy costs prompting elevated MoM inflation prints for July through to October, but thereafter we are looking for much softer readings. The squeeze on spending power from higher goods and energy prices could lead to cuts to discretionary spending that impacts the service sector and cools inflation faster there. At the same time the jobs market is cooling and wage inflation is slowing rather than rising, which is what allowed the supply shock post pandemic to morph into rapid inflation that nearly hit 10%. There is also evidence of softer housing-related inflation on the way with new tenant rents already turning negative. Housing accounts for around 40% of the core CPI basket by weight and that process will help inflation to return to 2% in 2026.

We don’t disagree with the market pricing of 50bp of cuts this year, but rather than 25bp moves in September and December, we are favouring a 50bp move in December followed by three 25bp cuts in 2026. This would be a similar playbook to the Federal Reserve’s actions in 2024, where they waited until being completely comfortable to commit to a lower interest rate environment.

Chair Powell should brace for some questions on liquidity management

In terms of liquidity circumstances, it’s quite a comfortable story. This stems back to the debt ceiling, which limits the ability of the US Treasury to engage in net issuance. That in turn means that tax income taken in by the US Treasury gets spent down quite quickly, resulting in a boost to bank reserves. In consequence, despite the quantitative tightening (QT) done, there is no sense of bank reserves dipping into a state where there might be liquidity issues. The lift of the debt ceiling would put all of this into reverse, resulting in a material reduction in bank reserves and tighter conditions. The glide path for QT already engineered by the Fed has set the stage for this, and in that sense the Fed is broadly ready. Chair Powell may or may not choose to comment on this at the press conference.

A juicier element that Chair Powell likely will be questioned on is the recent suggestion from Senator Cruz that the Fed should not compensate banks for holding (excess) reserves. Traditionally minimum bank reserves were a regulatory requirement that paid zero interest. But, because of the large QE engineered during the GFC, banks had to be compensated at market rate levels for holding more reserves than they really needed. That coincided with a change in the way the funds rate was managed, with the rate charged on bank reserves operating as something of a soft ceiling (IOER at 4.4% currently). And loosely, the effective floor rate is proxied by the rate set on the reverse repo facility (RRP at 4.25% currently). Both represent liquidity pools, and both are avenues that the Fed employs to help manage liquidity circumstances. Moreover, they are two Fed levers that help guide the funds rate within the policy floor and ceiling rates (currently 4.25% to 4.5%).

In our opinion, any decision that removes a rate compensation on bank reserves is certainly un-practicable, and potentially unworkable. Banks would then choose not to hold excess reserves, likely channeling liquidity excesses into reverse repo balances. But doing so would make the Fed’s liquidity management job more difficult. Chair Powell won’t bring this up voluntarily at the press conference, but it is likely a hot question that someone will ask.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post
