data.title: Rimini Street: Strategic Growth And Margin Strength
data.description:
Rimini Street (NASDAQ:RMNI) reported mixed Q1 FY25 results, with a small YoY revenue decline related to their gradual shut down of their PeopleSoft support business.

However, I believe the market is discounting the future growth opportunities this company has in store. Rimini is evolving to support a host of modern software solutions, and they are also doing so much more profitably than they have been able to in the past.

Rimini's partnerships with critical providers of enterprise tools is a powerful tailwind that I believe positions the company to generate substantial returns for investors over the next few years.

The stock appears meaningfully undervalued at the moment given their opportunities to scale, along with their recurring revenue model and improving margins. Given this context, I am initiating a BUY rating on Rimini Street based on the potential upside and the growing relevance of their business model.

Business Description & Fundamentals

Rimini Street's primary objective is to help companies extend the useful life of certain software systems (such as those offered by Oracle and SAP) through Rimini's affordable support offerings. Many vendors, including those listed above, do not continue to support older versions of their software offerings years after release, which forces customers to upgrade (whether they want to or not).

Rimini gives customers the option to avoid these costly and often disruptive upgrades by providing third party support for things like bug fixes, security updates, performance optimization, and system monitoring.

The Rimini ONE platform includes several layers such as Application Management Services (AMS) for overall system management, Third-Party Software Support to help customers avoid paying vendors for maintenance and upgrade support, and Interoperability Services through Rimini Connect which helps customers integrate older systems with modern cloud and hybrid cloud environments.

Rimini's primary offering is designed to help customers avoid upgrading for around 15 years, which frees up corporate IT budgets for other projects that can offer substantially higher returns on investment.

Rimini currently has 3,092 active clients as of the end of the first quarter, which was up from 3,040 a year ago. However, this figure is a little deceptive as it counts clients who've purchased more than one product multiple times. Their unique client count is currently 1,575, up from 1,535 a year ago. Rimini also has a plethora of large businesses as customers, with 71 Fortune 500 companies and 19 Fortune Global 100 companies, which I believe demonstrates the value they can provide to large organizations.

One of the biggest growth drivers for Rimini moving forward is their new partnership with ServiceNow (NOW). Rimini already assists clients with both onboarding and integrating with ServiceNow's platform as it can be tricky to set up on top of legacy ERP systems like Oracle or SAP. As part of this new partnership, ServiceNow is training its sales people to include Rimini in their go-to-market strategy, which should expand Rimini's reach to new customers.

The deal makes perfect sense as Rimini enables substantial cost savings for businesses in regards to their legacy systems, which frees up IT budgets to spend on more modern software tools (such as ServiceNow's platform for workflow automation). Rimini's CEO Seth Ravin noted on the earning call that this is one of the company's largest long term growth opportunities, and it makes sense to me given the synergies between the two companies.

Rimini has also joined forces with Workday (WDAY) and Rimini is now considered a certified application management services provider for them. This allows Rimini to support clients not just on older systems, but also on more modern platforms like Workday where Rimini can aid customers in lowering costs and improving system performance. Additionally, hundreds of Rimini's customers already use Workday so there is a natural upsell opportunity here.

Support sales related to VMware also spiked this past quarter, with many small and medium sized businesses turning to Rimini to avoid the huge price increases tied to VMware's new subscription based business model. This change came largely as a result of Broadcom's acquisition of VMware back in 2022. Following these changes, some companies saw support costs associated with VMware jump over 400%, and Rimini offers a much more affordable alternative which allows firms to continue using VMware infrastructure efficiently.

There are also a couple of risks that are worth monitoring when it comes to the fundamentals of Rimini's business. First off, macroeconomic uncertainty this past quarter has led to a decline in revenue retention as this figure declined 1% YoY to 88%. According to management, some customers chose not to renew their subscriptions due to both geopolitical tensions and a shift in their IT budget priorities towards other efforts (such as AI tools).

However, management is looking at this situation optimistically as they believe that if and when budgets tighten further, more IT departments will begin to view Rimini's low cost support model as an attractive alternative to paying for direct vendor support. Additionally, during past downturns (such as the Dot-Com bubble and the GFC) many customers stayed with Rimini through the lows, demonstrating the resilient nature of their business model.

One of the biggest risk factors for Rimini since the company went public has been the litigation risk from Oracle and other vendors they are undercutting on support prices. The Rimini ONE case has officially been resolved, however the Rimini II case is still an ongoing concern. So far, the courts have ruled that Oracle is not entitled to any financial damages, and Rimini is also actively pursuing recovery of $58.7 million worth of attorney's fees from Oracle.

However, management has suspended their guidance partially due to this legal risk which means it is something that investors should monitor. However, I believe this case is unlikely to have a materially negative impact on the company's financials at any point in the future.

Financials

Revenue in Q1 FY25 was down 2% YoY to $104.2 million compared to $106.7 million a year ago. This decline was primarily due to the slow wind down of Rimini's Oracle PeopleSoft support business which contributed only $7 million in revenue (7% of the total) compared to $8.2 million (8% of the total) a year ago.

The choice to exit this business has been debated extensively by executives at Rimini, however they reaffirmed their commitment to shutting this unit down in their most recent 10-Q filing. The rationale here makes sense given this segment supports a legacy product that itself is experiencing a structural decline in demand. Also, Rimini's legal battles with Oracle related to this product have been a major overhang on the stock for a number of years, which is another reason management is choosing to walk away.

While revenue declined, net income more than doubled compared to Q1 FY24, hitting $3.4 million this past quarter. This change was primarily due to improved operating cost controls and lower legal expenses compared to the prior year period. ARR was slightly disappointing, coming in at $396 million compared to $416 million a year ago. Management attributed this decline to higher customer churn as well as the decline in the PeopleSoft business.

Profitability metrics across the board were very strong, with gross margins improving 120bps YoY to 61%, mostly due to a $2.2 million decline in compensation expenses tied to delivering their services to customers. S&M expenses fell 12% YoY to $34.3 million, and this cost section now only accounts for 33% of revenue (a 400bps improvement YoY).

G&A expenses also declined 5% to $17.5 million, however management expects this line item to increase in Q2 FY25 due to investments in growth opportunities and higher regulatory costs.

Adjusted EBITDA for the quarter hit $15.3 million, which let to a substantial adjusted EBITDA margin increase of 470bps YoY. Billings, which are a forward looking indicator for companies with subscription business models, increased 7.2% YoY to $79.4 million. Additionally, if you exclude the decline in the PeopleSoft business, billings actually grew 11.3% YoY. This demonstrates strength in Rimini's new core suite of offerings, and I view this as an incredibly strong sign for the future of the company.

Finally, the company's liquidity position remains very solid. Rimini ended the quarter with $123.8 million in cash and cash equivalents and a net cash position of $27.1 million. While they are currently running a working capital deficit of ~$60 million, this is almost entirely due to their high current deferred revenue balance. Given deferred revenue acts as a source of cash in the near term, I view their balance sheet as very strong and this also gives Rimini the flexibility to offer great terms to customers to increase retention rates if we experience an economic downturn.

Valuation

In order to determine Rimini Street's intrinsic value, I created a discounted cash flow model. The perpetual growth rate used in the model was 2%, and my projection period covers the next 5 years. Also, the figures for cash, debt, FY24 financials, and shares outstanding were all pulled from S&P Capital IQ.

For my weighted average cost of capital I first computed the cost of debt using the company's TTM interest expense divided by the total debt balance as of the end of the most recent quarter. I then calculated the cost of equity using the yield on a 10-year U.S. treasury bond as my risk free rate, the stock's beta from Capital IQ, and the equity risk published on Professor Aswath Damodaran's website for the month of June.

My revenue growth rates were conservative, as I have projected a slight decline over each of the next two years to reflect the PeopleSoft wind down. After FY26 I have the top line returning to growth of between 1%-2% annually to reflect growth from the new partnerships with ServiceNow, Workday, and others.

For operating margins, I have modeled in no changes as the company is actively investing in new growth opportunities which may offset some of their cost controls they have implemented recently. I also assumed there will be very minimal taxes due over the projection period due to Rimini's $235 million accumulated deficit and the large NOLs they can utilize over the coming years.

For the cash flow statement items, I have D&A expenses declining modestly over the next few years. My reasoning here is that capital expenditures have been very low over the past 5 years, at around 0.9% of revenue annually, and so D&A should naturally follow suit. I also modeled the change in net working capital to be around 2% sales, which reflects recent trends as this figure has been positive over the last few years.

Based on these assumptions I arrived at an equity value of ~$612 million and an implied share price of $6.66 after dividing the equity value by Rimini's fully diluted shares outstanding. This represents sizable upside of around 97.8% based on the current market share price of $3.37 as of Thursday, June 12th.

Conclusion

While some of Rimini's financial results for the first quarter were underwhelming, I believe their most recent report underscored strength in a number of key areas. Between margin expansion, strong free cash flow generation, and progress related to key partnerships with powerful enterprise software providers, there is a lot to like here.

I believe the company's role as a cost saving engine for businesses with both legacy and modern IT infrastructure is a great niche to be operating in at the moment, especially given the current uncertainty regarding the economic outlook over the coming quarters.

Both the ServiceNow and Workday deals will likely allow Rimini to add a number of new clients, which should boost ARR and in turn the top and bottom line moving forward. Although there are still some risks related to the economy, customer retention, and the Oracle lawsuit, I believe they are minimal relative to the potential catalysts on the horizon.

My valuation model also implies that shares are worth substantially more than their current market value based on the present value of expected future cash flows. While these assumptions may not play out perfectly in reality, I cannot imagine shares stay in this range for much longer. I am very bullish on this stock at the moment, and therefore I am rating the stock a BUY.

data.title: Diamondback Energy: U.S. Oil Export Power Shines As Strait Of Hormuz Concerns Rise
data.description:
Diamondback Energy, Inc. (NASDAQ:FANG) opened up around 4% higher Friday morning, while the price of crude oil futures (CL1:COM) was up around 8%. Crude oil futures are currently up around 13% this week, making it one of the commodity's best weeks in recent years, back to about $73 per barrel. This spike came after Israel launched a strike on Iranian military and nuclear targets. Although President Trump has since pushed Iran to make a deal, the risk of escalation appears significant, as both sides consider various forms of retaliation and pressure.

Historically, I've seen Diamondback Energy as a hedge against this situation. I covered it last August in "Diamondback Energy: Increased Geopolitical Tensions Raise Free Cash Flow Outlook." My core view was that North American production giants like Diamondback have a specific edge because the ability of Europe and Asia to export and transport oil through the Strait of Hormuz safely is compromised or potentially at risk. In such a scenario, North American producers like Diamondback could lead the way in exports at a potentially much higher price.

That said, since economic growth factors have generally slowed since last year, the price of oil has declined. FANG is down approximately 20% since I last covered it, while oil prices were down about $5, but have since returned to about even since last August. FANG's valuation remains slightly low with a forward "P/E" of 11X and a forward dividend yield of 3.6%.

In my opinion, the company has an excellent track record of prioritizing shareholder value through accretive acquisitions and strategic development opportunities. Since last August, Diamondback's outlook has changed following its significant merger with Endeavor Energy. On the one hand, this deal has increased Diamondback's leverage while also improving its upside potential to higher energy commodity prices. Although we can still only speculate on the matter, I suspect that FANG may face a sharp Bull market if supply-side risk factors continue to arise, particularly geopolitical risks.

Understanding Oil's Hedge Potential

My position on FANG and oil in general is fundamentally a hedge against risks related primarily to the Middle East. This was explained in my final takeaway:

In my view, US producers, with their innate capacity to export abroad and lack of political risk (compared to the Middle East), make them an ideal portfolio hedge against oil price risk. Remember, a geopolitical oil price shock would hamper the economy while raising inflation, meaning stocks and bonds may decline in that scenario while tying up the Federal Reserve's ability to stimulate the economy. Thus, stocks like FANG are among the few that should offer strong hedges against this risk.

The need for such could not be more clear today. If oil prices rise sharply, not due to strong economic demand, but to a potentially massive decline in supply stability, we should see a combined rise in inflation and economic strain. However, if oil prices rise, pushing inflation higher, the Federal Reserve's ability to provide dovish quantitative easing stimulus may also be compromised. In such a scenario, I suspect that oil producers like FANG may be among the few equities to rise.

To me, the opportunity in FANG and other North American oil producers is not necessarily a means of increasing performance, but a unique hedge against unique risk that would be bearish for almost all other equity market segments. FANG is a hedge because the oil price exposure to geopolitical conflicts is highly asymmetrical. Iran exports around 1.3M barrels per day, or just over 1% of global consumption. Saudi Arabia accounts for approximately 6.6M, while the UAE is around 2.6M, Qatar around 1.5M, Iraq about 6.2M, and Oman is approximately 900K.

In my view, there is relatively high potential that some of Iran's oil infrastructure will be compromised. If the conflict escalates significantly, the Strait of Hormuz may close, threatening the ~30% of all seaborne crude that flows through the strait. Some of that capacity may be diverted, but not likely the majority of it. That would likely result in the most significant global oil supply shock ever, a similar decline to the forced cuts seen in 2020, but without a direct and considerable decrease in demand.

Furthermore, the U.S. currently lacks a substantial Strategic Petroleum Reserve, having depleted much of it to lower oil prices in 2022, thereby increasing the market's vulnerability to an oil supply crisis. Since the world would essentially become oil-price takers, unable to reduce consumption quickly, it is expected that oil prices could rise to $200 per barrel.

We must keep in mind that oil and gas are cheap today. Oil rose to nearly $150 in 2008 when consumer prices were significantly lower. The inflation-adjusted oil price is now back around its lowest level, excluding the COVID extreme, over the past twenty years:

At the 2008 peak prices, oil would be around $220 today, adjusting for inflation. Arguably, because oil production technology has improved over the past two decades, the cost of production has not risen as fast as inflation, meaning such a price is not necessarily likely. However, it remains true that oil is very cheap, enough that oil producers are not expected to profit significantly on newly drilled wells, even in the cheap and abundant Permian basin.

As a result of low prices, most producers in the U.S. and abroad are dramatically slowing output growth by slowing well drilling. U.S. domestic production remains very high, but has stagnated and may be peaking as drilling levels are so low, falling more sharply after the market correction two months ago:

At the least, I suspect that lower drilling activity limits Diamondback and other energy companies' exposure to a recessionary slowdown in oil demand. Unlike past slowdowns, oil producers appear to be very quick to reduce capital expenditures on expansion, concerned that the market may revert to a glut. Still, I suspect that drilling levels are unsustainably low, while I am not convinced that oil and gas demand will drop significantly in a recession. Keep in mind that improvements in vehicle mileage partly drove the 2008 decline in demand, while the 2020 decline in oil demand was due to lockdowns that directly limited global transportation. I think it's fair to assume U.S. oil and gas demand will not rise, but there are limited signals that it will decline soon.

Diamondback Growing Through Accretion

Like most oil and gas producers, Diamondback has scaled back its organic growth through drilling and exploration since the late 2010s. Its Capex-to-revenue remains elevated and did rise after its recent acquisition, but the company is not growing nearly as quickly through those means. See below:

Diamondback's growth has shifted from drilling and exploration to accretive acquisitions. In general, larger oil companies with greater economic scale have higher "P/E" valuations today than smaller exploration and development companies, creating accretive acquisition growth opportunities. Diamondback's revenue has risen dramatically over recent years, even adjusting for the rise in energy commodity prices. Its debt has also increased, but has remained proportional to its revenue growth:

During its last investor call, Diamondback's managers emphasized their efforts to slow down capital expenditures, aligning with the efforts of most other producers to derisk due to economic demand concerns. Its dividend will fall with prices in the sub-$60 range, but that is less of an issue now that oil is back above $70. Still, the company remains focused on using cash flows to reduce leverage and pursue share repurchases. Crucially, the call mentions that Diamondback holds the most considerable drilled-but-uncompleted well backlog in the Permian Basin, enabling it to maintain production by completing wells without incurring costly drilling expenses.

The "DUC" backlog across all U.S. oil producers was huge going into 2020. After the crash in production, many relied on these wells as a low-cost way of improving production. DUC well inventories are likely a significant reason why oil production has remained flat over the past two years, despite significantly lower drilling levels. However, the inventory of these wells was very low going into 2025, while the rig count, even in the Permian, continued to decline:

In my view, the combination of low drilled-but-uncompleted well inventories and lower drilling activity indicates a potential decline in U.S. production. This is generally anticipated to offset an expected economic-driven decrease in US. oil demand, but for now, we're not seeing that and may not, depending on tariff policies. In my view, it is most likely that U.S. oil demand, as well as global demand in general, will remain relatively stagnant, while various factors, including oil production cuts and a potentially extreme decline in Middle Eastern supply availability, may push supply levels significantly lower.

Diamondback is ideally suited for this scenario, as it can raise production quickly if prices rise. Diamondback is, to me, slightly undervalued, assuming oil and gas remain around current levels that ensure profitability. Being a U.S. company with vast reserves, I continue to see Diamondback having a huge potential upside from geopolitical risk factors that may significantly increase global demand for U.S. crude exports. Many other North American E&Ps also meet the criteria; however, combined with Diamondback's scale advantage and long history of shareholder value approach, it provides the best risk-reward profile among E&Ps.

The Bottom Line

We cannot know the odds of the Strait of Hormuz shutting down. Still, for me, the fact that we've seen a long-term escalation in Middle Eastern-related geopolitical issues over several years makes this scenario more likely than the market is currently anticipating. Ultimately, because this risk factor is bearish for most stocks and bonds, I think investors are best off focusing on the few equities with hedge potential. Although there are many E&Ps, Diamondback has, to me, the ideal tradeoff between stability and scalability potential while focusing more squarely on shareholder value.

Of course, there are other ways to hedge against the risk of a significant, geopolitically related rise in oil prices. The United States Oil Fund, LP ETF (USO) is a decent option, but it lacks the dividend potential of FANG. As oil is not currently far from breakeven, a substantial increase in oil prices would significantly improve its EPS. Diamondback's income outlook slid with lower profit margin expectations. Likely, with oil prices back up over $70 today, this will improve. See below:

Regarding the potential for the Strait of Hormuz to shut down, it is unclear if that is likely, and it's reasonable to assume it is not the most likely outcome. Some disruption to Middle Eastern oil supplies seems relatively likely given that Iran's oil facilities are viewed as a target. That said, even if it's less than 50% likely that a severe disruption scenario occurs, the potential upside in oil (which would slow the global economy) is likely to be far more extreme than the $5-$10 downside if the conflict is peacefully resolved.

The other risk to my bullish outlook on Diamondback is a severe economic slowdown. This is a widely anticipated risk factor, and one that Diamondback and its peers are preparing for by dramatically lowering CapEx and drilling activity. In my view, most E&Ps are overestimating the decline in oil demand that could occur if the economy falters. The decrease in the rig count is almost akin to that seen in early 2020, which, of course, was not driven by the economy, but by a direct global effort to reduce transportation activity. I suspect ongoing drilling reductions limit oil's recession exposure and may be sufficient to create an oil shortage regardless of geopolitical events.

Still, as we saw earlier this year, FANG is very likely to decline if the equity market crashes. However, unlike previous declines, I expect oil producers to prove less cyclical than assumed, with a potentially significant inflation hedge benefit.
